edHandoff.summary: 55-year-old male with DM2 and obesity presenting with 3 days of progressive left lower extremity redness, swelling, and pain. Now with streaking erythema from ankle to mid-calf, warmth, fever 102.4°F, HR 108, BP 112/68. WBC 16.8. No fluctuance on exam but very tender. Lactate 2.4. Given vancomycin 1.5g IV and NS 1L bolus. Marked borders with skin marker. Pain improved with morphine.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to medicine for IV antibiotics, serial exams, and monitoring for abscess/necrotizing infection.

sources.name: 2014 IDSA Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

variant.diagnosis: Severe Cellulitis with Early Sepsis (Purulent, MRSA Risk)

patient.communicationStyle: Waited days before coming in because he thought it would get better. Minimizes symptoms initially. Doesn't love hospitals. Will admit the leg is 'pretty bad' if pressed.
patient.emotionalState: Irritable from pain, somewhat reluctant patient

chiefComplaint.location: Left lower leg — ankle to mid-calf
chiefComplaint.onset: Small cut on ankle 5 days ago (from edging the lawn). Redness started 3 days ago around the cut, has been spreading upward.
chiefComplaint.severity: Pain 7/10. Worse with walking or touching.

present: fever and chills (started last night — 'shaking under the covers')
progressive erythema spreading from ankle up the leg
warmth and tenderness of affected area
edema of left lower leg (shoes don't fit)
small draining wound at original cut site (seropurulent drainage)

pmh: Venous stasis changes bilateral lower extremities
History of MRSA skin abscess (left thigh, 2 years ago, I&D + TMP-SMX)

socialHistory.woundCare: Did not clean the original wound. Covered it with a dirty bandage.

physicalExam.general: Overweight male in moderate discomfort, leg elevated on pillow

leftLowerExtremity.inspection: Erythema from lateral ankle extending to mid-calf (~15cm vertical spread). Borders marked with skin marker in ED. Edematous. Small 1.5cm laceration on lateral ankle with surrounding induration and seropurulent drainage. Linear red streaking (lymphangitis) extending toward popliteal fossa. Chronic venous stasis changes (hemosiderin deposition, lipodermatosclerosis) bilateral — worse on left. No bullae. No necrotic or dusky tissue. No crepitus.
leftLowerExtremity.palpation: Warm to touch. Tender to light palpation throughout erythematous area. No fluctuance. No palpable fluid collection. Dorsalis pedis pulse palpable but diminished.
leftLowerExtremity.function: Ankle ROM intact but limited by pain. Can dorsiflex/plantarflex. Sensation intact. Compartments soft.

physicalExam.rightLowerExtremity: Chronic venous stasis changes. No acute erythema or warmth. Serves as comparison.
physicalExam.lymph: Left inguinal lymphadenopathy — 2 tender, mobile nodes, 1.5cm

labs.note: Markedly elevated — useful for trending response to treatment
Mildly elevated — early sepsis. Recheck after fluids.
Poorly controlled — contributes to immune dysfunction and impaired wound healing

imaging.findings: No fracture. Soft tissue swelling over lateral distal leg. No subcutaneous gas. No periosteal reaction to suggest osteomyelitis.
imaging.impression: Soft tissue infection without osseous involvement or gas gangrene.

admittingOrders.antibiotics: Vancomycin IV (dosed by pharmacy for trough 15-20 — covers MRSA given history). Add piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h if concern for polymicrobial/deep space infection. If penicillin allergy is mild urticaria (childhood, no anaphylaxis), piperacillin-tazobactam can often be safely given — consider allergy consult or use meropenem as alternative.
admittingOrders.woundCare: Daily wound assessment by nursing. Measure and photograph erythema borders daily (compare to marked borders). Saline wet-to-dry dressing changes BID to ankle wound. Debride if necrotic tissue develops.
admittingOrders.elevation: Strict left leg elevation above heart level when in bed. Compression only after acute infection resolves.
admittingOrders.painManagement: Acetaminophen 1g IV/PO q6h scheduled. Hydromorphone 0.5mg IV q4h PRN for breakthrough. Avoid NSAIDs (renal risk, interaction with anticoagulation).
admittingOrders.diabetesManagement: Hold oral hypoglycemics. Insulin drip if glucose >250 persistently, otherwise basal-bolus with sliding scale (glargine 20U + lispro correction scale). Endocrine/diabetes consult for optimization. Target glucose 140-180.
admittingOrders.monitoring: Vitals q4h. Daily CBC, CRP (trending response). BMP daily (vancomycin nephrotoxicity monitoring). Vancomycin trough before 4th dose. Mark erythema borders at admission — recheck at 24 and 48 hours to assess spread vs regression. If SPREADING despite 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics, or if develops crepitus/bullae/necrosis → STAT surgical consult for possible nec fasc.
admittingOrders.dvtProphylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily (obese, immobile, infected — high DVT risk)
admittingOrders.other: Smoking cessation counseling. Diabetes education consult. Social work for home health wound care on discharge.

keyLearningPoints: Purulent cellulitis (drainage, prior MRSA) = cover MRSA. Non-purulent cellulitis = beta-hemolytic strep is most likely.
IDSA classification: mild (no SIRS), moderate (SIRS criteria), severe (failed oral, immunocompromised, or signs of deep infection). This patient has moderate-severe.
Mark the borders with skin marker at admission — this is the most reliable way to assess treatment response at 24-48 hours
Cellulitis should improve within 48-72 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics. If not improving or worsening → reassess for abscess, deep space infection, or necrotizing fasciitis
Necrotizing fasciitis red flags: pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, rapid spread, bullae, dusky/necrotic skin, hemodynamic instability despite treatment
Poorly controlled diabetes (A1c 9.2%) impairs neutrophil function and wound healing — glucose management is part of infection treatment
mustNotMiss: Necrotizing fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, hemodynamic instability — SURGICAL EMERGENCY)
Deep venous thrombosis (unilateral leg swelling + erythema — can mimic or coexist with cellulitis)
Underlying abscess (fluctuance, failure to improve — may need ultrasound or CT to identify)
Osteomyelitis (if bone is palpable through wound or if infection doesn't resolve with antibiotics)
commonPitfalls: Not marking borders — you can't objectively assess improvement without them
Covering only strep (cefazolin) when the patient has purulent drainage and MRSA history — needs vancomycin
Not obtaining wound culture — guides de-escalation when results return
Discharging before 48-hour reassessment — need to confirm borders are receding
Ignoring the diabetes — glucose >250 impairs immune function. Treat both.
Mistaking chronic venous stasis changes for spreading infection — compare to contralateral leg
teaching.attendingQuestions: The ED started vancomycin. Is that the right choice and why? When would you add Gram-negative coverage? And what's your 48-hour decision point — how will you know this is working or failing?
